Posing a Daz Genesis 9 Character in Unreal — The "Goal Celebration" Shot

Non-MetaHuman pipeline · Daz G9 FBX → Blender pose → Unreal Engine 5.8 (unreal-mcp bridge) · process notes, pitfalls, and the recipe that finally worked.
Daz Genesis 9Blender 4.5 (headless) Unreal 5.8unreal-mcpNo MetaHuman

1 · The goal & the setup

Take an existing Daz Genesis 9 character (Sherry, a plain textured FBX — no MetaHuman) and render her in an arms-overhead, open-mouth "goal celebration" shout pose, cleanly, inside Unreal Engine.

The dynamic pose itself was authored in Blender and baked to a 2-frame FBX animation (the in-engine euler Control Rig can't lift arms fully overhead). The mesh, skeleton, studio and capture pipeline already existed. The single blocker: applying that Blender animation to the character in Unreal made the whole mesh render invisible.

2 · The core problem

With the Blender-baked celebration anim applied via an AnimationSingleNode component, the character rendered zero pixels — not even a silhouette — even though the actor was present, visible, in-frustum, and reported valid ~160 cm bounds. Removing the anim → she rendered perfectly (ref pose). So the animation, not the mesh or lighting, was destroying the render.

Why the bounds lie

A SkeletalMeshComponent's bounds come from bone positions, which stay sane. The skinning matrices (pose × inverse-bind) are what deform the vertices — and those can blow the mesh to a degenerate blob while the bounds still read full size. "Valid bounds + zero pixels" is the signature of broken skinning, not a missing/hidden actor.

3 · Root cause — a BIND-POSE mismatch (not scale)

This is the key insight, and it took a long detour to reach:

  • SK_Sherry was imported from the original Daz Sherry.fbx — it carries the Daz bind/rest pose.
  • The celebration anim was authored in Blender, which re-derives the armature rest pose on import + apply-transform.
  • Playing a Blender-bind animation on a Daz-bind mesh makes the skin matrices explode → invisible.

Proof: the identical anim is invisible on SK_Sherry but renders a clean, correctly-scaled arms-up figure on a mesh re-imported from the same Blender FBX (matching bind). That single A/B test cracked it.

The fix in one sentence

Apply the Blender-authored animation only to a mesh that was imported from the same Blender FBX. Re-import the character standalone (its own skeleton + mesh + materials) — do not retarget the Blender anim onto the original Daz-imported mesh.

4 · What to AVOID (the false leads that burned time)

Don't assume it's purely a scale problem

Scale was wrong too (the Blender FBX bind sat at meter scale — hip ≈ 0.87 — while UE reads coordinates literally for anim-on-existing-skeleton, collapsing her to a ~1.6 cm blob). Fixing scale alone only changed "invisible" into "tiny collapsed blob." The real root was the bind mismatch. Chasing scale first cost the most time.

Don't use FBX global_scale / unit tricks to fix scale

Blender's exporter dumps global_scale into the FBX UnitScaleFactor (I saw it become 10000), or with FBX_SCALE_NONE leaves the coordinates untouched — neither reliably changes the numbers UE reads for an anim-on-existing-skeleton. The result is unpredictable collapse.

Don't manually multiply verts / edit-bones / fcurves ×100

Baking the scale straight into the geometry did make coordinates a literal 87 cm — but it broke the mesh (invisible even in ref pose) and desynced the animation. Manual geometry surgery is a trap.

Don't import a huge / heavily-edited FBX on a loaded editor

A ×100-scaled full FBX import hung the editor's game thread for ~13 minutes (1 core spinning on "Loading FBX Scene", MCP bridge dead) and forced a hard restart — which lost the unsaved studio setup. Keep imports light; the clean 42 MB rename-only full mesh imports fine (~4 min).

Don't trust the thumbnail renderer to judge scale

UE's asset-thumbnail renderer auto-frames the mesh, so a collapsed/tiny figure still fills the frame and looks fine. It's great for a reliable, well-lit sanity check of the pose, useless for the scale.

Don't use auto-exposure for stills, and don't expect MCP-added lights to work

Auto-histogram exposure flickers frame-to-frame (blows out or crushes depending on how much dark surrounds the subject) — use manual exposure on an unbound Post-Process Volume. And lights added over the MCP bridge do not illuminate the scene; only the level's own saved lights work, so re-aim the existing sun rather than adding one.

Don't reuse the original Daz mesh's materials by accident

A fresh standalone import comes in with raw Daz materials — flat DefaultLit "plastic" skin and opaque white eyes. That reads as washed-out and wrong. You must carry over the already-fixed materials (see step 4 below).

5 · The recipe that worked

1Re-export from Blender (headless), rename only — no scaling

Load the pose FBX, rename the Blender-duplicated bones (X.001 → X_001, and patch the matching action fcurve data-paths), keep all meshes, and export with standard settings. No manual vert/bone/fcurve scaling. This keeps the character at the correct ~160 cm in UE and keeps the animation consistent with its own bind.

bpy.ops.export_scene.fbx(
    filepath=DST, use_selection=False,
    global_scale=1.0, apply_unit_scale=False,
    apply_scale_options='FBX_SCALE_NONE',
    add_leaf_bones=False, bake_anim=True, bake_anim_use_all_bones=True,
    primary_bone_axis='Y', secondary_bone_axis='X',
    object_types={'ARMATURE','MESH'}, mesh_smooth_type='FACE')

2Import STANDALONE (own skeleton) with materials

Import the FBX without a target skeleton, with import_materials=True, import_textures=True, import_animations=True. You get a complete character (body + clothing + hair + eyes) + its own _Skeleton + _Anim, all sharing the Blender bind → the anim will apply cleanly. (Times out the MCP call but completes; watch the project log for "Waiting for skinned assets".)

3Spawn & apply its own animation

Spawn the mesh actor (origin, yaw 180 so she faces the −Y camera), set the component animationMode = AnimationSingleNode and animationData.animToPlay to the imported _Anim. It poses immediately in the level — no PIE needed.

4Carry over the fixed materials

Reassign the already-fixed material instances from your good character folder onto the new mesh's matching slots (the slot names match — same character):

  • Skin → the Subsurface versions of Head / Body / Arms / Legs
  • Eyes → Eye_Left / Eye_Right, and a translucent "clear moisture" material on EyeMoisture_Left / EyeMoisture_Right / Tear (kills the opaque white cornea)
  • Open mouth → Mouth / Teeth / Mouth_Cavity (visible during the shout)

5Fix the eyes (a UE FBX eye-bone quirk)

The eyes render rolled up (sclera-only) in UE even though the same pose shows forward-looking eyes in Blender — the l_eye / r_eye local rotation doesn't survive the FBX round-trip faithfully (the primary_bone_axis='Y' remap). Cheap fix, no full re-import:

  • In headless Blender, delete the l_eye / r_eye rotation fcurves from the action (they revert to rest = forward relative to the head).
  • Export a light body-only FBX (~2.8 MB) and import it onto the existing standalone skeleton so the new anim binds to your already-materialed mesh.
  • Swap the actor's animToPlay to the corrected _Anim. Pupils now read forward.

6Studio & lighting

  • Turn off the editor's background CPU throttle so off-screen captures aren't black: EditorPerformanceSettings.bThrottleCPUWhenNotForeground = false.
  • Re-aim the level's existing atmosphere sun (a DirectionalLight with bAtmosphereSunLight=true) to front-light her — she faces −Y, so aim the light to travel toward +Y (yaw ≈ 82–90, pitch ≈ −32). Don't add a new light (won't illuminate over MCP).
  • Use an unbound Post-Process Volume with manual exposure (AEM_Manual, bias ≈ −3.5 to −4.3) for a stable, repeatable result.

7Capture reliably (World-Partition level)

The photobooth is a World-Partition map. Move the editor viewport camera to the shot pose first (so WP streams the studio cells) — otherwise the off-screen capture is black. Capture over the MCP HTTP endpoint (curl → 127.0.0.1:8000/mcp, CaptureViewport with a captureTransform), decoding only the SSE data: lines.

8Post-process the frame

Two overlays can't be suppressed in-capture: the red "CachedLightingPreExposure clipped" warning (appears whenever exposure ≳ 12, which is unavoidable at a good exposure) and the orange cyclorama-box wireframe — and both cross her raised arms, so they can't simply be cropped out. Fix in post (PowerShell System.Drawing): interpolate across those thin horizontal bands (locate them with a red/orange pixel scan), then crop inside the box side-edges to a waist-up composition (removing the floor line, feet-sprite, gizmo and flag).

6 · Render / capture gotchas (reference)

SymptomCause / fix
Black off-screen captureEditor background-CPU throttle → set bThrottleCPUWhenNotForeground=false. Also move the editor camera to the shot (streams WP cells).
Character is a dark silhouetteBack-lit by the sun — re-aim the sun to travel toward her front (+Y).
Exposure blows out / crushes between shotsAuto-histogram is unstable. Use manual exposure.
Red "Exposure NN clipped" text over the subjectLumen cached-lighting overlay, not removable over MCP. Inpaint the thin band in post.
Orange box framing the studioCyclorama geometry wireframe. Crop inside its edges / inpaint the top edge.
Bulb / gear / flag icons on screenEditor billboard sprites — hide each light/sky actor's .Sprite + .BillboardComponent_0 (bVisible=false), or crop them.
Editor hangs on FBX importToo-heavy / over-edited FBX. Keep imports light; restart the editor if the game thread is spinning with no log progress.

7 · Known limitations

  • Skin renders lighter in UE than in Blender EEVEE. This is a consistent renderer/subsurface difference (the same lighter tone appears in earlier UE renders of this character), not a bug. To match the darker EEVEE look, push the skin material's base/subsurface darker or warm the key light.
  • Exposure still reads a touch bright. Lower the PPV manual bias further, or dim the sun/skylight and re-balance.
  • The clean hero framing is waist-up — a full-body frame reintroduces the floor line, the feet-sprite and more of the box wireframe to clean up.

8 · One-line takeaways

  • Invisible mesh + valid bounds = broken skinning (bind mismatch or degenerate scale), never a hidden actor.
  • A Blender-authored anim belongs on a Blender-imported mesh — never retarget it onto the original Daz-imported skeleton.
  • Don't fight FBX units; export clean (rename dupes, no manual scaling) and let UE read the coordinates as-is.
  • Keep FBX imports light to avoid game-thread hangs; save assets (they survive restarts) but expect unsaved level/studio state to be lost.
  • Manual exposure + re-aim the existing sun + camera-first WP streaming + post-process the overlays = a repeatable clean still.
  • Fix eyes by deleting the eye-bone tracks; carry over your fixed subsurface/eye materials — a fresh import always lands with raw Daz materials.
Generated from the Sherry celebration-shot session · Daz G9 → Blender → Unreal 5.8 (unreal-mcp). Result: D:\mydata\3dassets\Sherry_Celebration_UE.png. Reusable UE assets saved under /Game/CelebFull/.
